questions.question: Explain the steps involved in mapping an ER diagram to relational schema.
keyPoints: Map regular (strong) entity sets to relations
Map weak entity sets with owner primary key
Map binary 1:1, 1:N, and M:N relationships
Map multi-valued attributes to separate relations
Map composite attributes to simple components
Handle specialization/generalization
Define primary keys and foreign keys with constraints

questions.question: Differentiate between total participation and partial participation with examples in ER-to-Relational mapping.
keyPoints: Total participation: every entity must participate (NOT NULL foreign key)
Partial participation: entity may or may not participate (NULL allowed)
Example: Every Employee MUST have a Department (total)
Example: Employee MAY have a dependent (partial)

questions.question: How is a ternary relationship mapped to relational schema? Explain with an example.
keyPoints: Ternary relationship requires a separate relation
Include foreign keys referencing all three participating entities
Primary key is combination of all three foreign keys
Example: Project assigned to Employee using Skill - needs all three keys

questions.question: What are derived attributes? How are they handled in ER-to-Relational mapping?
keyPoints: Derived attributes are computed from other stored attributes
They are NOT stored in the database to avoid redundancy
Computed on-the-fly when needed using base attributes
Example: Age can be derived from DateOfBirth

questions.question: Explain the three approaches for mapping 1:1 relationships with an example of when each is suitable.
keyPoints: Foreign key approach: Add PK of one entity as FK in other
Merged relation: Combine both entities into single table
Cross-reference: Create separate junction table
Choose based on participation constraints and query patterns

questions.question: How do you map specialization and generalization to relational schema? Discuss different strategies.
keyPoints: Strategy 1: Single relation with type attribute for each subclass
Strategy 2: Separate relations for superclass and each subclass
Strategy 3: Only subclass relations with all attributes
Choice depends on overlapping/total/disjoint constraints

questions.question: Explain the mapping of binary recursive relationships with an example.
keyPoints: Binary recursive: entity relates to itself (e.g., Employee manages Employee)
Map as single relation with self-referencing foreign key
Foreign key references PK of same relation
Example: Employee(EmpID, Name, ManagerID) - ManagerID references EmpID

questions.question: Consider an ER diagram for a Library Management System with entities: Book, Member, Author, Publisher. Relationships: Member borrows Book (1:M), Book has Author (M:N), Book published by Publisher (1:N), Author writes Book (M:N). Also, Book has multi-valued attribute 'Subject' and Author has weak entity 'Award'. Map this ER diagram to relational schema with all appropriate keys and constraints.
keyPoints: Book(BookID, Title, ISBN, PubID) - FK to Publisher
Publisher(PublisherID, Name, Address)
Member(MemberID, Name, JoinDate)
Borrow(BorrowID, MemberID, BookID, BorrowDate, ReturnDate) - composite FK
Author(AuthorID, Name, Bio)
BookAuthor(BookID, AuthorID) - composite PK
Subject(BookID, Subject) - separate for multi-valued
Award(AwardID, AwardName, AuthorID) - FK to Author as part of PK

questions.question: Explain the complete algorithm for ER-to-Relational mapping. Include handling of: (a) Regular entities, (b) Weak entities, (c) All relationship types (1:1, 1:N, M:N), (d) Multi-valued and composite attributes, (e) Specialization/Generalization.
keyPoints: Step 1: Map regular entities - create relation with all simple attributes, choose PK
Step 2: Map weak entities - add owner PK as FK, composite PK
Step 3: Map attributes - composite to simple, multi-valued to separate relation
Step 4: Map 1:1 - FK in one relation or merge or junction
Step 5: Map 1:N - FK in N-side relation
Step 6: Map M:N - create new junction relation
Step 7: Map specialization - choose strategy based on constraints
Step 8: Define integrity constraints (NOT NULL, UNIQUE)

questions.question: Design an ER diagram for a Hospital Management System and map it to relational schema. Entities: Patient, Doctor, Ward, Treatment. Relationships: Doctor treats Patient (M:N), Patient admitted to Ward (1:N), Doctor assigned to Ward (1:N), Ward has WardBoy (1:N), WardBoy is weak entity of Ward. Also include specialization: Nurse (works in Ward, assigned to Patients). Show all relational tables with primary keys, foreign keys, and constraints.
keyPoints: Patient(PatientID, Name, DOB, Gender, Address)
Doctor(DoctorID, Name, Specialization, Salary)
Ward(WardID, WardName, Capacity, Type)
Treat(DoctorID, PatientID, TreatmentDate, Diagnosis) - M:N mapping
PatientAdmission(PatientID, WardID, AdmitDate)
DoctorAssignment(DoctorID, WardID)
WardBoy(WardBoyID, WardID, Name) - weak entity mapping
Nurse(NurseID, Name, WardID) - specialization entity

questions.question: Discuss the mapping of union types (categories) in ER-to-Relational mapping. When would you use different strategies? Explain with examples.
keyPoints: Union type: entity is subset of union of multiple entity types
Strategy 1: Create relation for union with type attribute and foreign keys to all constituent entities
Strategy 2: Create separate relation for each entity that is member of union
Strategy 3: Single table with all possible attributes (vertical splitting)
Example: VehicleOwner can be Person OR Company - use type attribute to distinguish
Choose based on: number of common vs specific attributes, query patterns, update frequency
